items.q: Can binary search be used on an unsorted list?
items.correct: No - binary search relies on the list being sorted so it knows which half to discard; on unsorted data it may throw away the half containing the target.
items.misconception: Yes - binary search works on any list because it always checks the middle.
items.random_wrong: Yes - binary search sorts the list while it searches.
items.why: The halving trick only works because order tells you which side the target must be on. No order, no guarantee - that is why the precondition matters.

items.q: Does one pass of bubble sort fully sort a list?
items.correct: No - one pass only guarantees the largest unsorted item bubbles to its correct end position; multiple passes are needed until no swaps occur.
items.misconception: Yes - bubble sort finishes after a single pass through the list.
items.random_wrong: Yes - bubble sort only ever needs to compare the first two items.
items.why: Students watch one pass tidy things up and assume completion. The algorithm repeats until a clean pass with zero swaps proves the list is sorted.

items.q: Does abstraction mean making a problem vaguer?
items.correct: No - abstraction removes irrelevant detail while keeping the essential features precise, like the London Underground map keeping stations and connections exact.
items.misconception: Yes - an abstract version of a problem is just a less accurate one.
items.random_wrong: Yes - abstraction means adding artistic decoration to diagrams.
items.why: Removing detail sounds like losing accuracy. Good abstraction is selective: everything needed for the task is kept exact, everything else is deliberately dropped.

items.q: Binary search can only be used when the list is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sorted. “Very short” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Repeatedly comparing neighbouring items and swapping them if they are out of order describes:
items.why: Correct answer: Bubble sort. “Binary search” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Removing unnecessary detail to focus on what matters
items.why: It's Removing unnecessary detail to focus on what matters. “Translating code into binary” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Checking each item in turn from the start until the target is found
items.misconception: Jumping straight to the middle item
items.random_wrong: Sorting the list first, then halving it
items.why: It's Checking each item in turn from the start until the target is found. “Jumping straight to the middle item” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In the worst case, how many items must a linear search check in a list of 20 items?
items.why: The real figure is 20 checks. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
